Much of this call, in its generalities if not its increasingly-ambitious sp=
ecifics, particularly in the enterprise space, could have been lifted from =
calls five years ago, with the talk about ecosystems, flywheels, and center=
ing Shopify as the hub of the many spokes in a merchant=E2=80=99s wheel.

On a somewhat related note, there was one bit in the call that I thought wa=
s notable, involving Shopify=E2=80=99s nascent effort to make Shop Pay avai=
lable on non-Shopify sites; Finkelstein said in response to a question abou=
t whether this was intended to be a beachhead for non-Shopify merchants:

Yes. I mean, you sort of nailed it, which is that this really allows us to =
begin a business relationship with brands and businesses and huge retailers=
 that may not otherwise be ready to fully migrate on to Shopify enterprise,=
 whether it=E2=80=99s Shopify Plus or it=E2=80=99s headless =E2=80=94 our H=
ydrogen product or even commerce components. But Shop Pay off-platform stil=
l very much in its early days, but we see that as really the tip of the spe=
ar for enterprise, and it allows us to begin a relationship.

But why is Shop Pay in particular so important? Well, right now, it is the =
highest converting checkout on the internet. More than a 100 million buyers=
 have opted into Shop Pay and it=E2=80=99s already =E2=80=94 I mean, the qu=
arter alone, it=E2=80=99s facilitated $12 billion of GMV and a $110 billion=
 cumulative since we launched it. So we know that Shop Pay is consumers=E2=
=80=99 favorite way to check out from an accelerated perspective. And now, =
really, what we=E2=80=99re trying to do is continue to make it easier to ch=
eck out with Shop Pay, add it into things like Shopify point-of-sale, for e=
xample, and expand it across more surfaces. But, really, making Shop Pay th=
e preferred accelerated checkout across commerce is the goal here.

And by creating this opportunity for some very large brands to use Shop Pay=
 off-platform, again, the goal is not for them just to use Shop Pay off-pla=
tform indefinitely. It=E2=80=99s to begin a business relationship. It=E2=80=
=99s the same philosophy around commerce components, right? Not everyone wa=
nts to use all of Shopify. There are some very large brands that historical=
ly didn=E2=80=99t consider Shopify that are now coming on, because they wan=
t to use our checkout, which converts 35% better than our competitor or 15%=
 better on average across the entire internet. They want to use Shop Pay or=
 they want to use something with our inventory. They want to use our admin.=
 They want to use point-of-sale. By allowing them to choose certain compone=
nts of Shopify, we believe over time, they will take more and more componen=
ts to us, and that=E2=80=99s what Shop Pay off-platform provides us with.

This obviously makes sense strategically for Shopify, and Shop Pay is a gre=
at product; what is interesting, though, is why this is even strategically =
possible. Go back to the =E2=80=9CBuy With Prime=E2=80=9D saga: Amazon want=
ed to own payments in part because they needed to control the return experi=
ence; Amazon=E2=80=99s ultimate customer is, well, customers, and they have=
 a brand to protect. Shopify=E2=80=99s customer, though, is merchants: they=
 just need to impress their customers with improved payment conversions; if=
 something goes wrong with delivery or returns, that=E2=80=99s not Shopify=
=E2=80=99s problem, or brand on the line.

Shop Pay, to be fair, is so successful precisely because it is a consumer-f=
acing brand and UX; it is, though, entirely under Shopify=E2=80=99s control=
, and, crucially, entirely software: that is much more in line with Shopify=
=E2=80=99s capabilities, obviously; what is less obvious is how efforts tha=
t don=E2=80=99t fit that investor-friendly =E2=80=9Casset-lite=E2=80=9D par=
adigm can also be operational and strategic constraints, and Shopify is bet=
ter off without them.
